title: West Africa Ro-Ro Corridor Briefing — March 2026 Week 4
summary: Week 4 March briefing: Lagos port congestion elevated, two ro-ro vessels inbound. Grimaldi ETA revised. Sallaum advisory on anchorage delays. USD/NGN remains volatile above 1,650. Full corridor risk assessment.
body: West Africa Ro-Ro Corridor Briefing — Week 4, March 2026. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY: The US–West Africa ro-ro corridor is experiencing above-average disruption in the final week of March 2026. Two key vessels are inbound to Lagos with near-simultaneous ETAs, which will intensify the already congested anchorage queue. Importers with April delivery commitments face elevated risk this week. VESSEL SITUATION: GREAT ABIDJAN (Sallaum) — en route, ETA Lagos 3–4 April. Currently tracking at 5.25°N, 3.85°E, speed 14.2kn. On current trajectory, arrives Lagos approaches 3 April. However, Sallaum has issued a port advisory warning of 4–6 day anchorage wait. Realistic berth date: 7–10 April. GREAT COTONOU (Grimaldi) — en route, ETA revised to 2 April. Currently 4.91°N, 2.44°E, speed 12.8kn. Expects Apapa discharge. Apapa anchorage wait: 3–5 days. Realistic berth: 5–7 April. PORT CONTEXT: Lagos (both Tin Can and Apapa) is congested due to end-of-quarter import surge, post-strike backlog, and NIMASA inspection delays. The simultaneous arrival of both vessels will stress pilot and tugboat services further. FX CONTEXT: USD/NGN at approximately 1,658 on the parallel market (CBN official rate: 1,601). Duty liabilities denominated in NGN are elevated. RISK RATING THIS WEEK: HIGH. Recommend all importers check PAAR status immediately.

title: Is Grimaldi Reliably Serving the Nigeria Corridor? — Carrier Performance Analysis Q1 2026
summary: Corridor Briefing analysis of Grimaldi's Nigeria service performance Q1 2026. On-time rate (within 48h of declared ETA): 58%. Average delay when late: 3.2 days. Main causes: vessel substitutions, Bight of Benin weather, Cotonou intermediate stop overruns.
body: Carrier Performance Analysis — Grimaldi Lines Nigeria Corridor, Q1 2026. Corridor Briefing has analysed Grimaldi's WAX service performance on the US–Nigeria lane for Q1 2026 (January–March). DATA: 4 completed voyages in the period. HEADLINE METRICS: On-time rate (within 48 hours of declared ETA at Lagos): 58% (2 of 4 voyages). Average delay when late: 3.2 days beyond declared ETA at the berth. Late reasons breakdown: Bight of Benin adverse weather: 2 voyages. Cotonou intermediate stop overrun: 2 voyages. Vessel substitution mid-voyage: 1 voyage (GREAT COTONOU substituted for GRANDE TEMA, January voyage). Lagos anchorage congestion (beyond carrier control): all 4 voyages impacted. COMPARISON: Sallaum Lines on the same corridor in Q1 2026: on-time rate 72%. ANALYST COMMENT: Grimaldi's lower on-time rate on this lane reflects two factors: (1) the Cotonou intermediate stop adds variability that Sallaum's direct service does not have; (2) the WAX service vessels are slightly smaller and older, with lower average speeds. For importers on tight downstream deadlines, we recommend building a 4-day buffer beyond Grimaldi's declared ETA at Lagos. Grimaldi is competitively priced and has good cargo care standards — the delay risk is real but manageable if planned around.

title: Easter 2026 Import Surge — What It Means for West Africa Port Congestion
summary: Easter 2026 falls on 5 April, generating an import surge at Lagos and Tema that peaks mid-March to early April. Port congestion is expected to remain elevated through 15 April. Importers with April arrivals should book customs agents now.
body: Easter 2026 Seasonal Analysis — West Africa Port Demand. Corridor Briefing tracks seasonal import patterns at West African ports. Easter 2026 (5 April) is generating the typical pre-holiday demand surge that arrives at Lagos and Tema approximately 3–6 weeks before the holiday. OBSERVED DATA (March 2026): Volume at Tin Can Island Terminal is running 18% above the Q1 2025 equivalent period. Apapa Quays is 12% above the same period. The surge is concentrated in consumer goods (containers) and vehicles (ro-ro). IMPACT ON RO-RO VESSELS: The simultaneous arrival of GREAT ABIDJAN and GREAT COTONOU in early April places both vessels in the highest-congestion window of the year. Historical data from Corridor Briefing's vessel event database: the Easter period (4 weeks spanning the holiday) has averaged 6.8 days of anchorage wait for ro-ro vessels at Tin Can since 2022, compared to 3.1 days in non-holiday months. CUSTOMS PROCESSING: NCS staffing is reduced during Easter (18–21 April). Importers whose vehicles are still in examination during this period will experience additional delays. RECOMMENDATION: Book your customs clearing agent and port agent no later than 14 days before your vessel's ETA. Ensure PAAR is filed and duty paid before 28 March to avoid the NICIS-2 maintenance window. For April arrivals, target vehicle collection before 15 April to avoid holiday processing slowdowns.

title: Bight of Benin Weather Window — April 2026 Forecast for Maritime Operations
summary: April 2026 weather outlook for the Bight of Benin shipping lanes. Early April: moderate swell (2–3m), wind 15–20kn. Mid-April: West African monsoon onset expected, potential for 3–4m swell and reduced visibility. Late arrivals may face speed reductions.
body: Bight of Benin Maritime Weather — April 2026 Outlook. Corridor Briefing compiles maritime weather context for the West Africa shipping corridor. The following is based on NOAA and ECMWF seasonal modelling, not a certified maritime weather forecast. EARLY APRIL (1–10 April 2026): Sea state: Moderate. Swell 2–2.5m from the south-southwest. Wind: 15–18 knots, consistent from the southwest. Visibility: Good. Recommended operational speed: normal. IMPACT ON INBOUND VESSELS: GREAT COTONOU and GREAT ABIDJAN are expected to be transiting the Bight of Benin approaches (approximately 5°N, 2–4°E) between 1–3 April. Early April conditions are favourable and speed reductions are unlikely. MID-APRIL (11–20 April): The West African monsoon onset is forecast for approximately 12–15 April 2026, which is slightly earlier than the historical average (18 April). Once established, expect: Swell 3–4m, wind 20–25 knots, intermittent squalls with reduced visibility. Vessels may reduce speed by 2–3 knots during squall periods. LATE APRIL ONWARDS: Monsoon fully established. Standard seasonal conditions. All carriers operate normally but may show 10–20% transit time variability. SUMMARY: The timing of the current inbound vessels (early April ETA) appears to be ahead of the monsoon onset. No weather-related delays anticipated for the current arrivals. Vessels sailing in the second half of April should plan for monsoon conditions.

title: Nigeria Ro-Ro Importer Handbook — Corridor Briefing Annual Edition 2026
summary: Annual reference guide for vehicle importers on the US–Nigeria ro-ro corridor. Covers carriers, transit times, customs process, typical delays, cost structure and pre-arrival checklist.
body: Nigeria Ro-Ro Importer Handbook 2026. CARRIERS ON THE US–NIGERIA LANE: Sallaum Lines (WA-1 Service): Baltimore → Cotonou → Lagos (Tin Can). Monthly sailing. Average transit: 14–16 days Baltimore to Lagos. Grimaldi Lines (WAX Service): Savannah/Baltimore → Cotonou → Lagos (Apapa). Monthly sailing. Average transit: 15–17 days. TYPICAL TRANSIT SUMMARY: Ocean transit: 14–16 days. Anchorage wait at Lagos: 3–7 days (seasonal). NIMASA inspection after berthing: 1–2 days. Discharge operations (ro-ro): 1–2 days. NCS customs examination: 2–5 days. Vehicle collection from terminal: 1 day. TOTAL FROM VESSEL DEPARTURE TO VEHICLE IN YOUR HANDS: 25–40 days depending on congestion and documentation. COST STRUCTURE (approximate): Ocean freight: $800–$1,500 per vehicle (varies by size, carrier, season). Import duty: 35–43% of CIF value. Terminal handling: ₦50,000–₦80,000 per vehicle. Customs agent fees: ₦100,000–₦200,000 per consignment. Demurrage (if incurred): ₦25,000–₦40,000 per vehicle per day after free days. PRE-ARRIVAL CHECKLIST: ☐ Form M obtained from bank. ☐ SON certification secured. ☐ CCVO issued by US chamber of commerce. ☐ PAAR filed on NICIS-2. ☐ Customs duty paid online. ☐ Port agent engaged at Lagos. ☐ Customs clearing agent briefed.

title: ETA Impact Playbook - What to Rebook When Lagos Arrival Slips by 48 Hours
summary: Corridor Briefing outlines what importers should re-check when a Lagos-bound vessel slips by 24-72 hours: customs appointments, truck dispatch, warehouse slots, and cash timing for duties.
body: ETA Impact Playbook - Operational Steps After a Slip. Importers often ask what a revised ETA actually changes. The answer is usually not just the date on the vessel card. A 48-hour slip into Lagos can ripple into customs scheduling, truck readiness, warehouse staffing, and cash timing for duties. WHAT TO RE-CHECK FIRST: 1. Customs broker availability and any pre-booked examination expectation. 2. Truck dispatch windows if you were planning collection close to berth. 3. Warehouse receiving slots or final-customer delivery promises. 4. Whether the shift pushes the berth window into a holiday, weekend, or known congestion spike. 5. Whether PAAR or duty funding is still on track relative to the new timeline. ANALYST VIEW: The highest-cost mistakes usually happen when importers treat an ETA revision as informational rather than operational. If the berth estimate also moves, every downstream commitment should be reviewed the same day.
